claims.claim_text: A Deployment with no readiness probe may route traffic to an unready Pod during rollout.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes documentation states that readiness probes determine when a container is ready to accept traffic and that failed readiness removes the Pod from matching Service endpoints.

claims.claim_text: A container runs as root by default if no securityContext is set.
claims.anchor_text: The container runs as root by default because no securityContext is set.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This Deployment does not set a `securityContext`, so you should verify whether the image runs as root or as a non-root user by default.
claims.rationale: Whether a container runs as root depends on the image and runtime settings, so the original sentence overstates a condition that is not universally true.

claims.claim_text: If resource limits are present but requests are omitted, scheduling predictability may be harmed.
claims.anchor_text: Resource limits are present, but requests are omitted, which may harm scheduling predictability.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Resource limits are present, but you should verify whether requests are explicitly set or defaulted by policy, because that affects scheduling assumptions.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes can default an omitted request to the limit for a resource, so the original sentence is too absolute to support as written.

further_reading.title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the rollout and traffic-routing behavior behind the module's readiness-probe review advice.

further_reading.title: Configure a Security Context for a Pod or Container
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains how Kubernetes securityContext settings affect runtime identity and privilege controls.

further_reading.title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides the canonical explanation of requests, limits, and how they influence scheduling and runtime behavior.

notes: Most of the module is pedagogical guidance and does not need inline citation under the stated density rule; the citation-worthy items are concentrated in the Kubernetes-specific example block.
